citation: Asercion, C. (2023). "i'm sorry did you say street magic" & there are names here more powerful than our own. GameRT Resources.
summary:
# Summary

The provided text is a descriptive resource document from 2023, authored by Caro Asercion, detailing two tabletop role-playing games (TTRPGs): *i'm sorry did you say street magic* and *there are names here more powerful than our own*. The document does not present empirical research or data analysis; rather, it functions as a promotional and informational guide, likely intended for library patrons or gaming communities associated with GameRT.

The primary focus is on defining the mechanics and scope of *i'm sorry did you say street magic*, which is characterized as a "GMless city-building story game." The text specifies that this game supports two to six players and has a duration of three hours or more. The core gameplay loop involves collaborative discovery and imagination, where players construct a city filled with neighborhoods, landmarks, and residents. A key mechanic highlighted is the discovery of "true names" and the intersection of these entities, allowing players to set events in motion that alter relationships within the constructed world.

The second title, *there are names here more powerful than our own*, is described as a "worldbuilder" and a sister game to the primary title. It is noted for its versatility, capable of being used as a standalone tool by one or more players or in conjunction with *street magic*. The text provides links to video recordings (VOD) of actual play sessions, offering visual evidence of the games in action, though no quantitative data from these sessions is analyzed.

A significant portion of the text is dedicated to contextualizing these games within a broader ecosystem of media and safety practices. It lists "Play Alikes," comparing the titles to other TTRPGs such as *Microscope* by Ben Robbins and *The Quiet Year* by Avery Alder, as well as video games like *Cozy Grove* and *Dorfromantik*. This suggests a thematic alignment with cozy, atmospheric, and narrative-driven experiences. Similarly, "Read Alikes" include works by N.K. Jemisin (*The City We Became*) and Neil Gaiman (*Neverwhere*), reinforcing the urban fantasy and mythic undertones of the games.

Crucially for library services, the text explicitly lists "Safety Tools," including *Script Change*, *Lines and Veils*, and *The Door is Open*. This indicates a deliberate emphasis on creating safe and inclusive gaming environments, a critical consideration for public library programming. The document also outlines the GameRT Twitch Policy, which mandates kindness, prohibits hate speech, and provides mechanisms for reporting harassment, further underscoring the importance of community standards in these spaces.

In summary, this text serves as a curated entry point for librarians and patrons interested in narrative-driven, GMless TTRPGs. It provides essential metadata regarding player count, duration, and thematic content, while also offering resources for safety and related media. The absence of empirical findings limits its utility for quantitative analysis, but it offers valuable qualitative insights into the design philosophy and community guidelines surrounding these specific games.
sourceText:
“i'm sorry did you say street magic” & there are names here more powerful than our own
by Caro Asercion
i'm sorry did you say street magic is a GMless city-building story game for two to six players, that runs three or more hours. Discover and imagine a city filled with life and vivid detail, packed with a myriad of neighborhoods, landmarks, and residents. Discover their true names, and the ways that they intersect—then set events in motion that will change or alter
their relationships. there are names here more powerful than our own is a worldbuilder, and a sister game to i'm sorry did you say street magic. It can be used in conjunction with street magic, or as a
standalone tool, by one or more players. Watch the VOD of this session here: The Game “i'm sorry did you say street magic” by Caro Asercion “there are names here more powerful than our own” by Caro Asercion Actual Play
● Let's Play: I'm Sorry Did You Say Street Magic PT1 | #GENERATIONFANTASY Articles
● Interview with Caro Asercion - First Person Scholar

City Vibes ● The Unsleeping City [Full Episodes] - YouTube
🍃 ● city blues. [lofi / jazz hop / chillhop mix]
● Floating City Chill Lofi Beats
Play Alikes ● TTRPGs ○ there are names here more powerful than our own by Caro Asercion ○ Beak, Feather, & Bone by Tyler Crumrine ○ Companions Tale by Laura Simpson/Sweet Potato Press ○ Epitaph by lessthanthreegames ○ A Mending by Shing Yin Khor ○ Microscope by Ben Robbins ○ The Quiet Year by Avery Alder
● Video Games ○ Cozy Grove bySpry Fox LLC ○ Dorfromantik by Toukana Interactive
Read Alikes ● The City We Became by N.K. Jemisin ● Neverwhere by Neil Gaiman ● Piranesi by Susanna Clarke ● The Name of the Wind by Patrick Rothfuss ● Wayfarers Series & Monk and Robot Series by Becky Chambers
Safety Tools ● Script Change by Beau Jágr Sheldon ● Guest Blog: An Introduction to Lines and Veils ● The Door is Open by Eirik Fatland ○ Blog by Lizzie Stark ○ Blog 2 by Eirik Fatland ● TTRPG Safety Toolkit collected by Kienna Shaw and Lauren Bryant-Monk ○ TTRPG Safety Toolkit Guide ○ Google Drive

Game RT Twitch Policy 1. Be kind and respectful to one another 2. Hate speech of any type will not be tolerated 3. We reserve the right to remove anyone from the chat for any reason 4. If you are feeling harassed by anyone, please notify a GameRT twitch mod and one of us will assist you 5. If you'd rather talk privately about anything, please send us a direct message
